description: The Forestry Commission increases the value of woodlands to society and the environment. Forestry Commission is a non-ministerial department, supported by 2 agencies and public bodies .

details.body:
The Forestry Commission increases the value of woodlands to society and the environment.

Forestry Commission is a non-ministerial department, supported by 2 agencies and public bodies.

image.alt_text: close up of a tree tube witha young oak tree planted in it

ordered_featured_documents.summary: Find information and support on woodland creation in England, including funding to create woodland, legal requirements and best practice for sustainability.
ordered_featured_documents.title: Tree planting and woodland creation: overview

ordered_featured_documents.summary: Find information on sustainable woodland management in England, including funding to maintain your woodland, and regulations you need to follow.
ordered_featured_documents.title: Managing and maintaining woodlands: overview

ordered_featured_documents.summary: How the ‘Keep it Clean’ tree health campaign continues to help protect our woodlands and forests from pests and diseases 10 years on.
ordered_featured_documents.title: Celebrating 10 years of our ‘Keep it Clean’ tree health campaign: how simple actions protect our trees

ordered_featured_documents.summary: Guidance on applying for a licence to fell growing trees using our Felling Licence Online service.
ordered_featured_documents.title: Apply online for a felling licence

ordered_featured_documents.summary: A calendar of online and face-to-face Forestry Commission events, and details of shows and exhibitions we are attending.
ordered_featured_documents.title: Forestry Commission events

image.alt_text: A dog in an English woodland. Photo by Laura Roberts on Unsplash.

ordered_featured_documents.summary: Forestry Commission grants for planting trees in England. Find out how to get funding for woodland creation, management and maintenance, and tree health.
ordered_featured_documents.title: Where to find grants for trees and woodland

ordered_featured_links.title: Identify a tree pest or disease

details.secondary_corporate_information_pages: Read about the types of information we routinely publish in our Publication scheme. Our Personal information charter explains how we treat your personal information. Read our policy on Social media use.

role_appointments.title: Baroness Barbara Young - Forestry Commission Chair

image.alt_text: Professor Arthur (Jo) Bradwell MBChB, DSc, FRCPath, FRCP, FICFor (Hon)

details.body:
Forestry Commissioners have a number of specific statutory duties and powers, summarised in the Forestry Act as:

  • promoting the interests of forestry
  • the development of afforestation
  • the production and supply of timber and other forest products

In practice, these mainly relate to providing incentives (grants), regulation though controls on felling, plant health issues and managing the public forest estate.

The Commissioners have a legal duty to seek a reasonable balance between the production and supply of timber and the interests of conservation. In promoting the multiple benefits of forestry, they also seek to take careful account of people’s needs and wishes, including local communities.

role_appointments.title: Professor Arthur (Jo) Bradwell MBChB, FRCP, FRCPath - Forestry Commissioner

ordered_board_members.title: Professor Arthur (Jo) Bradwell MBChB, DSc, FRCPath, FRCP, FICFor (Hon)

The HM Land Registry Board Chair works closely with the Chief Executive and Chief Land Registrar in leading the development of the organisation. The role has responsibility for ensuring that proper governance practices are followed and performance is monitored effectively.

details.body: The Chief Scientist at the Forestry Commission is responsible for ensuring that Forest Research provides the scientific knowledge and expertise needed to achieve sustainable forest management.
